edges.description: Regulatory dosage effects modify the cardiac, immune, endocrine, and neurodevelopmental expressivity of the deletion.
edges.source: Multigene dosage and miRNA regulatory disruption

edges.description: Impaired neural crest migration results in failure of outflow tract septation.
Reduced thymic tissue leads to decreased T-cell output and immunodeficiency.
Parathyroid hypoplasia causes hypoparathyroidism and low serum calcium.
Altered cortical development and catecholamine/proline metabolism are plausible contributors to the elevated psychosis risk in 22q11.2DS.

nodes.description: Neural crest cells from the occipital somites migrate through pharyngeal arches 3, 4, and 6 to contribute to aorticopulmonary septation and great vessel remodeling. TBX1 deficiency impairs neural crest cell migration and survival through non-cell-autonomous mechanisms, as TBX1 is expressed in pharyngeal mesoderm rather than neural crest cells themselves.

biological_processes: cardiac neural crest cell migration involved in outflow tract morphogenesis

evidence.snippet: congenital heart disease, particularly conotruncal malformations (ventricular septal defect, tetralogy of Fallot, interrupted aortic arch, and truncus arteriosus)

nodes.description: Neuropsychiatric risk arises from multiple dosage-sensitive mechanisms in the deleted interval. RANBP1 loss disrupts cortical progenitor proliferation and layer 2/3 projection-neuron generation in models, while COMT and PRODH dosage can alter proline-dopamine interactions and prefrontal cortical function in children with the deletion.

evidence.reference_title: Ranbp1, Deleted in DiGeorge/22q11.2 Deletion Syndrome, is a Microcephaly Gene That Selectively Disrupts Layer 2/3 Cortical Projection Neuron Generation.
evidence.snippet: Ranbp1 ... is included within the DiGeorge/22q11.2 Deletion Syndrome (22q11.2 DS) critical region ... Ranbp1(-/-)-dependent proliferative deficits substantially diminish the frequency of layer 2/3, but not layer 5/6 cortical projection neurons.

evidence.reference_title: Proline affects brain function in 22q11DS children with the low activity COMT 158 allele.
evidence.snippet: COMT activity and proline levels may therefore be altered in 22q11DS individuals ... elevated proline negatively affects brain function by an increase in dopamine in the prefrontal cortex. 22q11DS patients with low dopamine catabolic capacity are therefore especially vulnerable to this functional disruption.

nodes.description: Neonatal hypocalcemia due to parathyroid hypoplasia occurs in 50-60% of patients and may cause seizures.

evidence.snippet: serum ionized calcium every three to six months in infancy, every five years through childhood, every one to two years thereafter, preoperatively and postoperatively, and regularly during pregnancy

nodes.description: The typical proximal deletion removes dozens of protein-coding genes and non-coding regulatory elements. Beyond TBX1 dosage, haploinsufficiency of genes such as DGCR8 and CRKL alters miRNA biogenesis, epigenetic regulation, and modifier pathways, helping explain marked inter-individual variability.
nodes.id: Multigene dosage and miRNA regulatory disruption

evidence.reference_title: Understanding the Variability of 22q11.2 Deletion Syndrome: The Role of Epigenetic Factors
evidence.snippet: Typical miRNA expression patterns have been identified in 22q11.2DS, due to an alteration in miRNA biogenesis, affecting the expression of several target genes.

evidence.reference_title: Understanding the Variability of 22q11.2 Deletion Syndrome: The Role of Epigenetic Factors
evidence.snippet: The review of the literature confirms the hypothesis that the 22q11.2DS phenotype results from a network of interactions between deleted protein-coding genes and altered epigenetic regulation.

nodes.description: The parathyroid glands derive from the third and fourth pharyngeal pouches. Defective pouch development causes parathyroid hypoplasia, leading to hypoparathyroidism and hypocalcemia. Neonatal hypocalcemia may present with seizures and is often the presenting feature.

biological_processes: neural crest cell differentiation involved in parathyroid gland development

evidence.snippet: serum ionized calcium every three to six months in infancy

nodes.description: Meta-analysis estimates psychotic disorders in approximately 11.5% of individuals with 22q11.2DS and schizophrenia in approximately 9.7%, with higher prevalence in adult samples.

evidence.reference_title: Prevalence and incidence of psychotic disorders in 22q11.2 deletion syndrome: a meta-analysis.
evidence.snippet: schizophrenia (9.70%, 95%CI:6.50-14.20). Prevalence was significantly higher in

nodes.description: Severe T-cell immunodeficiency occurs in complete DiGeorge syndrome; partial immunodeficiency and variable T-cell lymphopenia are more common.

evidence.reference_title: Chromosome 22q11.2 Deletion (DiGeorge Syndrome): Immunologic Features, Diagnosis, and Management.
evidence.snippet: The clinical presentation of 22q11.2DS is highly variable particularly with

nodes.description: The thymus derives from the third pharyngeal pouch. Impaired pharyngeal pouch development leads to thymic hypoplasia or aplasia, resulting in reduced T-cell production. The immunodeficiency is typically partial, with low but functional T-cell numbers, though complete thymic aplasia (complete DiGeorge syndrome) occurs in ~1% of cases.

biological_processes: neural crest cell differentiation involved in thymus development

evidence.snippet: The major clinical manifestations of 22q11.2DS include congenital heart disease, particularly conotruncal malformations (ventricular septal defect, tetralogy of Fallot, interrupted aortic arch, and truncus arteriosus), palatal abnormalities (velopharyngeal incompetence, submucosal cleft palate, bifid uvula, and cleft palate), immune deficiency, characteristic facial features, and learning difficulties.
